description: This lecture frame shows a slide being presented during a Zoom meeting. The slide is titled "Signal" and "Fourier transform" and contains two main graphs side-by-side. The left graph illustrates a combined signal function `x₁(t)+x₂(t)` in the time domain, while the right graph shows its corresponding Fourier transform `X₁(f)+X₂(t)`. Below these main graphs are smaller, detailed views demonstrating how a signal can be sampled and reconstructed.
topic_title: Minimizing Aliasing with Filtering: Averaging Filter

label: cue
description: This lecture frame shows a presentation slide with the professor's video feed in the bottom right corner. The slide illustrates a concept related to **signal processing**, specifically demonstrating how a signal composed of two different frequencies can be separated using a **Fourier transform** and **filters** (represented by H(f)).
topic_title: Minimizing Aliasing with Filtering: Averaging Filter

label: cue
description:
This lecture frame shows a slide being presented on a screen. 

The slide illustrates the concept of a Fourier transform, comparing a time-domain signal (`x₁(t)+x₂(t)`) with its frequency-domain representation (`X₁(f)+X₂(t)`). It also demonstrates how a filter (`H(f)`) can be applied in the frequency domain to isolate or remove specific frequency components from a signal.
topic_title: Minimizing Aliasing with Filtering: Averaging Filter

label: cue
description: This is a lecture slide displayed during a Zoom meeting. The slide presents a diagram illustrating the concept of **Fourier transforms** and **filtering**. It shows how a composite signal (combining two individual signals) is broken down into its frequency components by the Fourier transform, and then how a filter H(f) can be applied in the frequency domain to isolate or remove specific frequency components, reconstructing a modified signal.
topic_title: Minimizing Aliasing with Filtering: Averaging Filter

label: start
description: This lecture frame shows a slide titled "EXAMPLES USING FILTERS IN COMPRESSION," with the professor visible in a small window at the bottom right. The slide discusses audio filtering, explaining that the human ear only detects frequencies up to ~20KHz, and how pre-filtering a signal with a low-pass filter at 20KHz and then sampling at 40KHz can significantly reduce data rates (from 3 Mbs to 640 Kb/s).
topic_title: Aliasing Minimization in Image Resampling

label: start
description:
This lecture frame shows a split view. In the lower right, a professor is visible, appearing to speak while gesturing. The majority of the screen is a hand-drawn diagram on what looks like a whiteboard or digital canvas.

The diagram illustrates concepts related to video signals and television standards. It depicts the transmission of video signals with horizontal and vertical sync, mentions electrical frequencies (60Hz and 50Hz) leading to NTSC, PAL, and SECAM standards, and explains interlaced scanning where one frame is split into odd and even fields. On the right, three factors are listed: synchronization, TV screen phosphor, and the introduction of color.
topic_title: YUV Subsampling Techniques (4:4:4, 4:2:2, 4:2:0/4:1:1)
